title: Serverless function → Postgres connection strategy — V kills H2

nodes.content: A serverless function (Lambda / Workers / Cloud Functions) needs to query Postgres on each invocation. Pick a connection strategy.
Traffic is bursty: 0 to 500 concurrent invocations within a few seconds. Average function runtime is around 100ms. Connection setup over TLS is 50-200ms.
Postgres has a hard connection ceiling (default around 100). Every invocation needs a connection. Connection churn at burst is the failure mode to design for.
Operational complexity matters. Adding a new component to the path (a proxy) introduces failure modes, observability surface, and another thing to upgrade.
Open a fresh connection per invocation. Simplest possible. Pays the 50-200ms TLS handshake every time.
Maintain a connection pool inside the function's container. Reuse connections across warm invocations of the same instance.
Put a connection proxy (RDS Proxy / PgBouncer) in front of Postgres. Functions talk to the proxy; the proxy multiplexes onto a small number of actual Postgres connections.

H2 silently fails at burst. Each cold-start container gets its own pool, so 500 concurrent invocations create 500 separate pools — each holding N connections. That is exactly the pattern the pool was meant to prevent. Looks fine in dev; breaks at scale.

H3 trades one operational component for predictable connection multiplexing. The proxy is the standard answer for this exact scaling shape, and the failure modes (proxy down, proxy lag) are well-understood.

H3 (connection proxy). H1 is acceptable for low-throughput functions but adds tail latency. H2 is the trap — feels right, fails on the burst case it's supposedly designed for. The smart-sounding option silently fails on its target case.
